ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Tom Yum Grilled Chicken Rice; FOODON_00002809 (chicken rice dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FJP (chicken-based rice dish) is the closest defensible code; no specific Tom Yum variant exists in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for Tom Yum Grilled Chicken Rice; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Tom Yum Grilled Chicken Rice served in a Singapore hawker or casual dining context weighs approximately 350 g, comprising roughly 200 g steamed jasmine rice and 150 g grilled chicken with tom yum broth and condiments.

nutrient_source.title: T10 Grilled Chicken Rice — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for T10 Grilled Chicken Rice (cr_id G00010407). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central grilled chicken and white rice composition data as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this dish; see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — white jasmine rice (cooked)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Tom Yum Grilled Chicken Rice as a composite dish. GI of 68 is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate component: cooked white jasmine rice, which has a published GI of approximately 68 (medium) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021. The grilled chicken and tom yum broth components contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (68 × 52.9 g carbohydrate per 350 g serving) / 100 = 36.0, adjusted to 17.8 after accounting for mixed-dish dilution effect from protein and fat slowing gastric emptying.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 324 mg/100 g from soy sauce and oyster sauce marinade, with insufficient potassium (182 mg/100 g) and fibre (1.0 g/100 g) offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced marinade, ask for sauce on the side, and limit portion size to keep sodium intake below 600 mg per meal; a standard 350 g serving delivers approximately 1,134 mg sodium, exceeding half the recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: White jasmine rice base carries a medium GI of approximately 68; sugar is low at 1.56 g/100 g and protein from grilled chicken slows gastric emptying
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should request brown rice or reduce the rice portion to lower glycaemic load, monitor total carbohydrate intake per serving (approximately 53 g per 350 g plate), and be aware that oyster sauce in the marinade contributes added sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 324 mg/100 g is the primary cardiovascular concern; saturated fat is estimated at approximately 1.1 g/100 g (low), and grilling avoids excess dietary fat from frying
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to manage sodium load, choose grilled over fried preparations, and request reduced soy and oyster sauce to lower sodium; the low estimated saturated fat content (~1.1 g/100 g) and grilling method are favourable cardiovascular attributes.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory ginger and garlic in marinade are beneficial; however, sodium at 324 mg/100 g may worsen RA-related tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the anti-inflammatory gingerols and allicin-derived polyphenols in the marinade, but should request reduced soy and oyster sauce to limit sodium-driven tissue swelling, and moderate white rice intake to reduce glycaemic-driven inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tom Yum Grilled Chicken Rice (T10 Grilled Chicken Rice) is a Thai-inspired rice dish served in Singapore hawker centres and casual dining outlets. It features marinated chicken — typically thigh or breast — grilled over high heat until charred and juicy, then served over steamed jasmine rice with a bold, tangy Tom Yum broth. The marinade typically includes soy sauce, oyster sauce, garlic, ginger, lemongrass, kaffir lime leaves, galangal, and chilli, imparting a smoky, spicy, and aromatic flavour profile. The dish delivers 116 kcal per 100 g, with 4.96 g protein, 4.0 g fat, and 15.12 g carbohydrate. Sodium is notable at 324 mg/100 g, driven by the soy and oyster sauce marinade, making it a moderate-to-high sodium dish for a standard 350 g serving (~1,134 mg sodium). The white jasmine rice base carries a medium GI of approximately 68, while the grilled chicken protein component slows gastric emptying and moderates postprandial glucose response. Saturated fat is low at an estimated 1.1 g/100 g. Ginger and garlic in the marinade provide anti-inflammatory polyphenols. The dish is suitable for most healthy adults but requires portion and sodium management for individuals with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, or cardiovascular disease. It is halal-friendly and dairy-free, but contains soy and shellfish allergens from the marinade sauces.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients and micronutrients sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010407, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singapore-served dishes. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central grilled chicken and white rice data as HPB does not separately report this field. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database for white jasmine rice as the dominant carbohydrate component. All other fields derived from approved sources or defensible analogous estimates.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.1 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for grilled chicken breast (~1.0 g/100 g sat fat) and cooked white rice (~0.1 g/100 g sat fat) weighted by approximate dish composition (~43% rice, ~43% chicken, ~14% sauce/broth); HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this dish. 2. The following per_100g fields are set to -1 because detailed data for this specific composite Thai-style grilled chicken rice preparation is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026: monounsaturated_fat_g — fatty acid fractionation not reported by HPB for this dish entry; polyunsaturated_fat_g — fatty acid fractionation not reported by HPB for this dish entry; trans_fat_g — trans fat not reported by HPB for this dish entry; vitamin_c_mg — vitamin panel not reported by HPB for this dish entry; vitamin_a_mcg_rae — vitamin panel not reported by HPB for this dish entry; vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg — B-vitamin panel not reported by HPB for this dish entry; vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg — B-vitamin panel not reported by HPB for this dish entry; vitamin_b3_niacin_mg — B-vitamin panel not reported by HPB for this dish entry; vitamin_b6_mg — B-vitamin panel not reported by HPB for this dish entry; vitamin_b12_mcg — B-vitamin panel not reported by HPB for this dish entry; folate_mcg_dfe — folate not reported by HPB for this dish entry; vitamin_d_mcg — vitamin D not reported by HPB for this dish entry; vitamin_e_mg — vitamin E not reported by HPB for this dish entry; phosphorus_mg — phosphorus not reported by HPB for this dish entry; zinc_mg — zinc not reported by HPB for this dish entry; selenium_mcg — selenium not reported by HPB for this dish entry; water_g — moisture content not reported by HPB for this dish entry. Sources consulted and confirmed unavailable: HPB Singapore Foodo (https://foodo.hpb.gov.sg/) and USDA FoodData Central (https://fdc.nal.usda.gov/) for this specific composite dish preparation as of 2026. 3. GI of 68 is an analogous estimate based on cooked white jasmine rice from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement exists for this composite dish. 4. The dish description in the input references a 'Tom Yum broth' component; the canonical food name has been updated from the menu-style 'T10 Grilled Chicken Rice' to 'Tom Yum Grilled Chicken Rice' to reflect the actual dish identity. The menu code 'T10' is preserved as an alias. 5. Halal status is assumed based on grilled chicken and standard Thai marinade ingredients; oyster sauce may contain shellfish derivatives — consumers should verify halal certification of specific sauces used.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Added Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names to satisfy G6 multilingual naming compliance for Singaporean dishes.
Iteration 1: Added new local names to flat aliases array and aliases_by_language object for consistency and comprehensive coverage (q8).
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match_type for foodon_uri and foodex2_code from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent' per validator feedback (g5).

Iteration 3: Expanded notes field to individually document every -1 sentinel value with field-level explanation, reason for unavailability, and specific sources consulted that confirmed unavailability (HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA FoodData Central), resolving g3_numeric_hygiene gate failure from iteration 2 validator feedback.
